insight: When deciding whether to hold or click a Feral (Cynestra), compare what it kills against what your opponent kills if they breach for 1. If both answers are 'drone,' hold the Feral. Additionally, consider whether the burst is replicable next turn; if not, attack with it.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion about when to hold versus click a Feral/Cynestra in combat decision-making.

insight: Antima Comet into Arka is generally weak when the Antima only kills low-value targets (e.g., 7 engineers worth). A large Antima that kills multiple powerful frontline units (e.g., 2 shredders) before buying Arka may be correct, but a small Antima leaves you severely over-defended.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about the viability of small Antima Comet into Arka transition.

insight: Rhino has several distinct use cases: 'gambit rhino' to threaten 1 extra damage, 'bridge rhino' to enable multicolor openings by going red first, 'anti-frontline rhino', 'soak rhino', 'peak rhino' timed to expire when soak rhinos become necessary, and 'option rhino' against inefficient threat.
author: .bky_1556
context: Discussion about common strategic uses of Rhino for a planned Reddit article.

insight: The primary justification for buying Rhino is to avoid being breached when you cannot otherwise hold drones. Casually buying Rhino without a specific tactical purpose is generally a mistake; expert mrguy888 summarizes this as 'don't buy Rhino' unless forced by the threat of breach.
author: mrguy888
context: Community debate on Rhino's value, with mrguy888 arguing strongly against casual Rhino purchases.

insight: Gambit Rhino is most effective in two cases: (1) against very inefficient threat such as Frostbite, where the opponent spending 2 bites to make you absorb on Rhino instead of Wall is a fine trade; and (2) when the opponent has a very valuable rearmost defender. If a Frostbite freezes the Rhino, the freeze was wasted; if it doesn't, you get to click it.
author: .bky_1556
context: Detailed breakdown of Rhino gambit use cases during a community discussion.

insight: Against 2 Drakes, the generally correct defensive response is 1 Forcefield + 1 Rhino.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of specific Rhino use cases including defending against Drakes.

insight: In Rhino mirrors, you want to be the player clicking two Rhinos to kill your opponent's prompt Rhino so you can hold less defense. One killed prompt Rhino saves one Rhino of your own.
author: .bky_1556
context: Analysis of Rhino mirror dynamics during a community discussion on Rhino usage.

insight: Attack performs a similar function to economy because it forces the opponent to buy defense, effectively hurting their income. However, attack is more efficient than drones and is limited by the opponent's absorb barrier. Economy is preferred until attack pressure exceeds the absorb barrier.
author: namington
context: Theoretical discussion about attack versus economy trade-offs during a replay review.

insight: When your opponent is out of soak and you have spare resources, buying attack rather than economy is nearly always correct because you want to end the game before exhausting your own soak supply, even if the available attack is relatively inefficient (e.g., Rhino).
author: .bky_1556
context: Late-game decision analysis during a replay review discussing attack vs. economy choices.

insight: Gauss Cannon can theoretically handle a P1 T1 Animus into Immolite rush by using Conduit. At some point Gauss Cannons pick off each wave of Immolites as they come off cooldown while also threatening Drones, with a Forcefield to block new Immolites while the Conduit player buys Engineers to stabilize.
author: .bky_1556
context: Theoretical discussion about countering an Immolite rush with Gauss Cannons.

insight: Apollo has a useful role in sets where it can snipe Blastforges to remove Drake threat, and will also ban Cynestra. Even if most units in the random set have 4+ health, Apollo can always snipe Walls.
author: .bky_1556
context: Set analysis discussion about the value of Apollo in various board states.

insight: In base+Scorchilla sets, the Scorchilla player is expected to win because the absorb denial from synced Scorchillas is too powerful. Syncing Scorchillas (attacking every other turn with groups) denies absorb 2 out of 3 turns. A semi-sync pattern (2x Scorch, 2x Tarsier, 2x Scorch) is likely better than fully syncing all Scorchillas.
author: mtanzer
context: Theoretical discussion about optimal Scorchilla strategy in a base+Scorchilla set.

insight: In base+Apollo+Scorchilla, Tarsiers are likely the correct primary attacker because Scorchillas are more vulnerable to Apollo than Tarsiers, discouraging red players from going green for Scorchillas when Apollo threatens them. Apollo does not play well in base+Apollo sets generally.
author: mqp
context: Theoretical discussion about optimal strategy in base+Apollo+Scorchilla set.

insight: With Ebb Turbine available, the typical recommended line is DD/DD/DDB followed by buying the Turbine, rather than DD/DB/DEbb. One Ebb Turbine is almost always correct to buy; a second Ebb is only good if you have a specific plan (e.g., Evore, Cauterizer, or Antima Comet to spend the extra blue). Never buy a third Ebb Turbine.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion about optimal Ebb Turbine opening lines and how many to buy.

insight: With two Ebb Turbines, you end up only wanting to click one per turn, leading to floating resources. One Ebb Turbine can be happily clicked every turn. If you genuinely need blue on exactly turn 4 for a specific purchase, go DD/DB to access it, but skipping Ebb is usually game-losing.
author: mrguy888
context: Analysis of Ebb Turbine opening efficiency discussing one vs. two Ebb Turbines.

insight: Plasmafier is a better 'second Ebb Turbine' than actually buying a second Ebb Turbine. Synth and Redeemer together are a case that incentivizes going DD/DB/Ebb to enable Synth+DD on the following turn.
author: mrguy888
context: Discussion about when to consider a second Ebb Turbine versus alternatives.

insight: With Lancetooth and Fission Turret available, DD/DC as P1 is preferred because early attack buys more attackers rather than getting absorbed. A second Fission Turret early in that position is a mistake; DD is far better than floating 2 gold.
author: mrguy888
context: Set reading discussion about a blue-green set featuring Lancetooth and Fission Turret.

insight: Hannibull's click ability is especially strong in low-economy situations where you aim to prevent the opponent from ever accumulating 7 gold. Allowing the opponent to kill 2 Engineers and a Drone can be correct when you have Hannibull absorbing, since those Engineers lose value once Hannibull is in play.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion about the strategic value of Hannibull's click ability.

insight: Master Bot does not effectively exploit opponent mistakes in complex sets. In a reviewed game (iIHFt-Nc6gh), the player made numerous serious errors including wasted Frostbites, buying multiple unused Blastforges, and misusing resources, yet still won comfortably, suggesting Master Bot fails to punish suboptimal play in high-complexity sets.
author: p0lari
context: Replay review where a new player made major errors against Master Bot but still won easily.

insight: Master Bot plays suboptimally in complex sets with many advanced units (b+11). A rated ~2000 player (p0lari) noted having no idea how to play a b+11 set, implying Master Bot in such sets is not a reliable teacher or strong opponent, and new players should use simpler sets (b+8) when learning.
author: p0lari
context: Discussion where p0lari reviewed a new player's loss against Master Bot in a b+11 set.

insight: Master Bot failed to correctly prioritize buying Arka in a set where it was clearly the dominant unit, even after p0lari (a ~2000-rated player) noted forgetting about Arka themselves when attempting to play the set. This suggests Master Bot may miss dominant late-game unit purchases in complex sets.
author: p0lari
context: p0lari attempted to play the same set as the player they were coaching against Master Bot and also forgot about Arka.

insight: Forcefield supply can run out in very long games, particularly in sets with Dgrid or Resonator mechanics where no efficient attackers are available and everything gets bought. Players should monitor Forcefield supply in Resonator sets.
author: consensus
context: Community discussion about surprising situations where base unit supply runs out.
